Munga is a silk yarn spun with the broken tussar filament. Silk textile is crafted from the natural protein fiber produced by silkworms during the process of silk production. Known for its luxurious feel, lustrous appearance, and smooth texture, silk has been prized for millennia. This exquisite fabric is used in high-end fashion garments, accessories, home furnishings, and decorative items, reflecting its status as a symbol of elegance and sophistication across cultures worldwide.

Banarasi products are characterised by brocade borders & pallus. They often have woven butas or Jaal on the body. Luxurious weaves with an intense color scheme are their specialty, along with jewel-toned brocades, zari, quality silk, and lavish embroidery.This resplendent banarasi design is handpicked from skilled craftsmen and one of the finest in the country.
Buta and Butis are names given to motifs in Indian traditional textiles. A small motif on its own is called a Buti.  Butas are larger than butis.

title: Handblock Bagru Print Handwoven Saree

productDetails.heading: Product Details
items: Hand block-printed textiles may exhibit characteristic imperfections such as slight variations in print alignment, color intensity, and pattern registration, which are inherent to the traditional artisanal techniques used in the printing process. Additionally, colors may fade or bleed over time due to the natural dyes and pigments employed. These variations contribute to the unique beauty and individuality of each hand-printed piece and should be embraced as part of its artisanal charm. This is a genuine handwoven piece, slight unevenness in the selvedge and weave may be observed, which is inherent to the manual weaving process. Additionally, colors may fade or bleed over time due to the traditional dyeing methods employed. These characteristics contribute to the unique authenticity and artisanal quality of each handwoven textile and should be appreciated as part of its inherent charm and beauty.

Handblock printing is a centuries-old art form that involves using hand-carved wooden blocks dipped in dye and stamped onto fabric by skilled artisans. Each block is meticulously carved with intricate designs, resulting in unique patterns and motifs on the fabric. This traditional technique allows for the creation of beautiful textiles with a distinct handmade aesthetic
Bagru process of printing involves hand block printing using natural and other dyes through traditional process practiced in the town of Bagru in West Bengal

On handloom, fabrics are woven entirely by hand without the use of electric power, embodying a tradition that spans centuries. The weaving process demands a harmonious blend of motion and rhythm from the weaver, resulting in the creation of distinct and authentic textiles. Each handloom product bears the mark of the artisan’s skill and dedication, offering a glimpse into the rich heritage of handcrafted textiles.

title: Traditional Saree with Sequins

productDetails.heading: Product Details
items: Completion of a single saree takes two to three days of work. Several regions have their own traditions of handloom saree. The most traditional are made in West Bengal

Under the royal guidance the tant (specially?jamdani) and?muslin?became famous in and around?Dacca?(now?Dhaka, Bangladesh) and?Murshidabad?(now?West Bengal, India) in the?Mughal era.[2]?British government tried to destroy this art to protect the textile industry of?Manchester, but the tant culture managed to survive.

Weaving of tant sari is famous and an age old crafting of?Bengal. The craftsmen deftly weave the cotton to thread which is woven to tant sari. Two shuttles are used for this purpose. Traditionally, handlooms were used by the weavers, which have today been largely replaced by power looms to weave these saris.

title: Handblock Bagru Print Handwoven Saree with Contrast Border

productDetails.heading: Product Details
items: Hand block-printed textiles may exhibit characteristic imperfections such as slight variations in print alignment, color intensity, and pattern registration, which are inherent to the traditional artisanal techniques used in the printing process; additionally, colors may fade or bleed over time due to the natural dyes and pigments employed; these variations contribute to the unique beauty and individuality of each hand-printed piece and should be embraced as part of its artisanal charm; this is a genuine handwoven piece, slight unevenness in the selvedge and weave may be observed, which is inherent to the manual weaving process; additionally, colors may fade or bleed over time due to the traditional dyeing methods employed; these characteristics contribute to the unique authenticity and artisanal quality of each handwoven textile and should be appreciated as part of its inherent charm and beauty.

Handblock printing is a centuries-old art form that involves using hand-carved wooden blocks dipped in dye and stamped onto fabric by skilled artisans; each block is meticulously carved with intricate designs, resulting in unique patterns and motifs on the fabric; this traditional technique allows for the creation of beautiful textiles with a distinct handmade aesthetic
Bagru process of printing involves hand block printing using natural and other dyes through traditional process practiced in the town of Bagru in West Bengal

On handloom, fabrics are woven entirely by hand without the use of electric power, embodying a tradition that spans centuries; the weaving process demands a harmonious blend of motion and rhythm from the weaver, resulting in the creation of distinct and authentic textiles; each handloom product bears the mark of the artisan’s skill and dedication, offering a glimpse into the rich heritage of handcrafted textiles.
